content.text: After a dark launch runs shadow traffic through a new AI system, you have raw numbers from both environments. The work of comparing those numbers accurately — choosing the right statistical tests, interpreting effect sizes, and translating findings into a promotion decision — is what dark launch metrics comparison is about. Done well, it converts a qualitative gut feeling into a defensible, reproducible engineering signal.
What It Is

Dark launch metrics comparison is the structured process of collecting performance, quality, and cost measurements from both a production system (the baseline) and a shadow system (the candidate under test), then applying statistical methods to determine whether the candidate is materially better, equivalent, or worse than the baseline.

The comparison happens across multiple dimensions simultaneously: raw latency numbers tell you about speed, LLM-as-judge scores tell you about output quality, token counts tell you about cost trajectory, and error rates tell you about reliability. A candidate must pass gates on all dimensions before it is considered ready for promotion.
Baseline collection: capturing stable production metrics over a representative window (typically 7–30 days)
Shadow collection: mirroring the same requests through the candidate and capturing its responses and resource usage
Statistical comparison: applying paired tests, effect size calculations, and confidence intervals to each metric dimension
Decision synthesis: combining the per-metric signals into a single go/no-go recommendation
Why It Matters

AI systems degrade in subtle ways that infrastructure health checks do not catch. A new model might produce slightly more verbose responses that inflate token costs by 30%, or a prompt change might increase refusal rates by 2% — neither of which surfaces in CPU or memory dashboards. Without rigorous metrics comparison, teams promote changes that look good in staging but quietly erode business value in production.

The non-determinism of generative models compounds the difficulty. The same input produces different outputs across runs, which means naive point-in-time comparisons give false confidence if the sample window is too short or the test statistic fails to account for variance. Statistical rigor is not optional — it is the only mechanism that can separate real signal from noise in an LLM output distribution.
Benefits
Objective promotion criteria: removes opinion from rollout decisions and replaces it with quantified confidence
Multi-dimensional coverage: simultaneously evaluates quality, latency, cost, and safety, preventing single-metric gaming
Early regression detection: identifies subtle regressions before they affect users, with zero user exposure risk
Reproducible audit trail: statistical test results document why a candidate was promoted or held, enabling post-launch reviews
Calibrated iteration: effect size data tells teams how much improvement to expect, informing prioritization of prompt tuning vs model upgrades
Trade-offs
Cost: running shadow traffic doubles inference spend during the evaluation window
Complexity: quality metrics like LLM-as-judge require a secondary LLM call and must themselves be calibrated against human ratings
Sample size requirements: detecting small effect sizes (d < 0.2) at 80% power requires hundreds to thousands of paired samples
Shadow fidelity: shadow responses are discarded, so user feedback loops (thumbs up/down) are unavailable as a quality signal
Time pressure: thorough statistical comparison adds days to a release cycle, creating friction with fast-moving teams
How It Works

The comparison pipeline has three stages. First, the baseline window is established: production metrics are collected over a stable reference period, long enough to include natural traffic variation (weekends vs weekdays, load spikes) but recent enough to exclude pre-existing regressions. Seven days is a common baseline window for steady-state systems.

Second, shadow traffic runs through the candidate system in parallel. A traffic mirror duplicates each incoming request and sends a copy to the shadow stack. The shadow's responses are captured, scored, and logged but never returned to the user. Timing, token counts, error codes, and quality evaluations are all recorded per request.

Third, a comparison engine runs the statistical tests. Latency distributions are compared using a paired t-test, since each request serves as its own control. Effect sizes are expressed as Cohen's d. Quality scores are compared with the Wilcoxon signed-rank test when score distributions are non-normal. Cost deltas are computed deterministically from token count differences.
Key Metric Categories
Latency Distribution

Do not compare means alone. Users experience tail latency, and a model that improves p50 while doubling p99 is a net negative. Compare p50, p95, and p99 independently. For AI workloads, time-to-first-token (TTFT) and inter-token latency matter separately from total generation time.
Response Quality

Quality comparison for generative AI requires multiple complementary signals because no single score captures all quality dimensions. LLM-as-judge provides a holistic rating but costs an additional inference call. Embedding cosine similarity measures semantic faithfulness to reference answers. ROUGE-L captures n-gram overlap for extractive tasks. Human evaluation, while expensive, is the gold-standard calibration signal for the automated metrics.
Error Rates and Tool Call Patterns

Track hard errors (API failures, timeouts, malformed outputs) and soft errors (refusals, incomplete task completions, hallucination detections). For agentic systems, also compare tool call frequency, tool selection accuracy, and tool chain length — a new model that selects redundant tools increases cost without improving output quality.
Token Usage and Cost

Compare input tokens, output tokens, and total cost per request. A model upgrade that improves quality by 5% but increases cost by 40% may not be economically viable at scale. Track cost per successful task completion, not just raw cost per call.
Architecture
The diagram below shows the full dark launch metrics comparison pipeline, from dual metric ingestion through statistical analysis to the promotion decision.

attrs.alt: Dark launch metrics comparison pipeline: production and shadow ingestion, statistical comparison engine, and decision output

content.text: Statistical Methods
Paired t-test for Latency

Because both systems process the same requests, a paired t-test is appropriate. The test statistic is computed from per-request latency differences (shadow latency minus production latency). A statistically significant negative difference means the shadow system is faster. A p-value threshold of 0.05 is standard; lower thresholds (0.01) are appropriate when the cost of false promotion is high.
The formula for the paired t-statistic is:
t = mean(d) / (std(d) / sqrt(n))
where
d[i] = shadow_latency[i] - prod_latency[i]
and n is the number of paired observations.
Cohen's d for Effect Size
Statistical significance alone does not tell you if a difference is practically meaningful. Cohen's d expresses effect size in units of standard deviations:
d = mean(d) / std(d)
. Conventional thresholds: |d| < 0.2 = negligible, 0.2–0.5 = small, 0.5–0.8 = medium, > 0.8 = large. A latency improvement with p < 0.05 but d = 0.05 is statistically real but practically irrelevant.
Confidence Intervals

Report 95% confidence intervals for every metric delta. A CI of [-120ms, -80ms] for p99 latency improvement is more informative than a single-number delta. CIs also reveal the worst-case bound: a CI of [-5ms, +200ms] means you cannot rule out a 200ms regression with 95% confidence, which is a reason to hold the rollout.
When to Use It
Before promoting any model version change to production, regardless of staging test results
After significant prompt engineering changes that alter response structure or length
When changing tool sets or tool invocation policies in an agentic system
When migrating to a different inference provider or changing temperature and sampling parameters
Any time a business stakeholder requires documented evidence that a change does not degrade user experience
How to Implement

The following Python script collects shadow and production metrics from a database, runs statistical comparison across all metric categories, and outputs a comparison report with a binary PROMOTE or HOLD recommendation.

import pandas as pd
from scipy import stats
import numpy as np

def compare_shadow_vs_production(db_conn, start_ts: str, end_ts: str) -> dict:
    """
    Compare shadow system metrics against production baseline.
    Returns per-metric comparison results and a promotion recommendation.
    """
    query = """
    SELECT
        request_id,
        system,          -- 'production' or 'shadow'
        latency_ms,
        input_tokens,
        output_tokens,
        error_flag,
        quality_score    -- LLM-as-judge 1-5, NULL if unscored
    FROM request_metrics
    WHERE ts BETWEEN :start AND :end
    """
    df = pd.read_sql(query, db_conn, params={"start": start_ts, "end": end_ts})

    prod = df[df.system == "production"].set_index("request_id")
    shadow = df[df.system == "shadow"].set_index("request_id")

    # Align on shared request_ids (paired observations)
    shared = prod.index.intersection(shadow.index)
    prod, shadow = prod.loc[shared], shadow.loc[shared]

    results = {}

    # --- Latency comparison ---
    latency_diff = shadow.latency_ms - prod.latency_ms
    t_stat, p_val = stats.ttest_rel(shadow.latency_ms, prod.latency_ms)
    d = latency_diff.mean() / latency_diff.std()  # Cohen's d
    ci = stats.t.interval(0.95, df=len(latency_diff)-1,
                          loc=latency_diff.mean(),
                          scale=stats.sem(latency_diff))
    results["latency"] = {
        "mean_delta_ms": round(latency_diff.mean(), 1),
        "p99_delta_ms": round(
            np.percentile(shadow.latency_ms, 99) - np.percentile(prod.latency_ms, 99), 1
        ),
        "p_value": round(p_val, 4),
        "cohen_d": round(d, 3),
        "ci_95": [round(ci[0], 1), round(ci[1], 1)],
        "pass": p_val > 0.05 or d > -0.2,
    }

    # --- Error rate comparison ---
    prod_err = prod.error_flag.mean()
    shadow_err = shadow.error_flag.mean()
    results["error_rate"] = {
        "production": round(prod_err, 4),
        "shadow": round(shadow_err, 4),
        "delta": round(shadow_err - prod_err, 4),
        "pass": (shadow_err - prod_err) < 0.01,
    }

    # --- Quality score comparison (Wilcoxon for non-normal distributions) ---
    scored = prod.quality_score.notna() & shadow.quality_score.notna()
    if scored.sum() > 30:
        _, qp = stats.wilcoxon(
            shadow.quality_score[scored], prod.quality_score[scored]
        )
        quality_delta = (shadow.quality_score[scored] - prod.quality_score[scored]).mean()
        results["quality"] = {
            "mean_delta": round(quality_delta, 3),
            "p_value": round(qp, 4),
            "pass": quality_delta >= -0.1,
        }

    # --- Cost comparison ---
    INPUT_CPT  = 0.000003   # cost per input token
    OUTPUT_CPT = 0.000015   # cost per output token
    prod_cost   = prod.input_tokens * INPUT_CPT   + prod.output_tokens * OUTPUT_CPT
    shadow_cost = shadow.input_tokens * INPUT_CPT + shadow.output_tokens * OUTPUT_CPT
    cost_delta_pct = (shadow_cost.mean() - prod_cost.mean()) / prod_cost.mean() * 100
    results["cost"] = {
        "delta_pct": round(cost_delta_pct, 1),
        "pass": cost_delta_pct < 20,
    }

    results["recommendation"] = (
        "PROMOTE"
        if all(v.get("pass", True) for v in results.values() if isinstance(v, dict) and "pass" in v)
        else "HOLD"
    )
    return results


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import json, sqlite3
    conn = sqlite3.connect("metrics.db")
    report = compare_shadow_vs_production(conn, "2024-01-10", "2024-01-17")
    print(json.dumps(report, indent=2))

The script pairs requests by request_id to enable paired statistical tests. Cohen's d accompanies every p-value, and the binary PROMOTE/HOLD recommendation derives from per-metric pass/fail gates defined before the dark launch begins.
AI Response Quality Comparison

Quality comparison requires a separate evaluation layer. LLM-as-judge uses a stronger model to rate both production and shadow responses on the same rubric, producing comparable numeric scores. The judge prompt must specify evaluation criteria explicitly to reduce inter-request score variance.

from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

JUDGE_PROMPT = """You are evaluating AI assistant responses. Rate the response on a 1-5 scale.

Criteria:
- Accuracy (is the information correct?)
- Completeness (does it fully address the question?)
- Conciseness (appropriately brief without omitting key information?)
- Helpfulness (would a user find this genuinely useful?)

Question: {question}
Response: {response}

Output ONLY a JSON object: {{"score": <1-5>, "rationale": "<one sentence>"}}"""

def judge_response(question: str, response: str) -> dict:
    result = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": JUDGE_PROMPT.format(question=question, response=response)
        }]
    )
    import json
    return json.loads(result.content[0].text)

def compare_quality(pairs: list[dict]) -> dict:
    """
    pairs: [{"question": ..., "prod_response": ..., "shadow_response": ...}]
    Returns mean score delta and whether shadow quality is acceptable.
    """
    prod_scores, shadow_scores = [], []
    for p in pairs:
        prod_scores.append(judge_response(p["question"], p["prod_response"])["score"])
        shadow_scores.append(judge_response(p["question"], p["shadow_response"])["score"])

    from scipy import stats
    delta = [s - p for s, p in zip(shadow_scores, prod_scores)]
    _, p_val = stats.wilcoxon(shadow_scores, prod_scores)

    return {
        "prod_mean":   round(sum(prod_scores)   / len(prod_scores), 2),
        "shadow_mean": round(sum(shadow_scores) / len(shadow_scores), 2),
        "mean_delta":  round(sum(delta) / len(delta), 2),
        "p_value":     round(p_val, 4),
        "n_samples":   len(pairs),
        "pass":        sum(delta) / len(delta) >= -0.1,
    }
Building a Comparison Dashboard

A metrics comparison dashboard should show time-series overlays of production and shadow metrics on the same axis, so reviewers can see whether shadow performance diverges from production over time (which might indicate distribution shift or model warm-up effects).
Latency percentile charts: overlay p50, p95, p99 lines for both systems with color-coded bands
Quality score distribution: side-by-side histograms with the Wilcoxon p-value annotated
Error rate time series: rolling 1-hour error rates for both systems on the same axis
Cost delta chart: cumulative token cost difference over the evaluation window
Statistical significance indicators: traffic-light badges showing current p-value and effect size
Sample size counter: real-time count of paired observations with the minimum required threshold marked
Examples

A comparison report generator provides a formatted, human-readable summary of the statistical analysis and can exit with a non-zero status code to block CI/CD pipelines when the recommendation is HOLD.

# metrics_report.py  — CLI comparison report generator
import argparse, json, sqlite3
from compare_metrics import compare_shadow_vs_production

def render_report(result: dict) -> str:
    lines = ["=" * 60, "DARK LAUNCH METRICS COMPARISON REPORT", "=" * 60]
    for key, val in result.items():
        if key == "recommendation":
            continue
        lines.append(f"\n[{key.upper()}]")
        for k, v in val.items():
            lines.append(f"  {k:<20}: {v}")
    rec = result.get("recommendation", "UNKNOWN")
    lines.append("\n" + "=" * 60)
    lines.append(f"RECOMMENDATION: {rec}")
    lines.append("=" * 60)
    return "\n".join(lines)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--db",    default="metrics.db")
    parser.add_argument("--start", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--end",   required=True)
    args = parser.parse_args()

    conn = sqlite3.connect(args.db)
    result = compare_shadow_vs_production(conn, args.start, args.end)
    print(render_report(result))
    # Exit non-zero if HOLD so CI/CD pipelines can gate on it
    import sys
    sys.exit(0 if result["recommendation"] == "PROMOTE" else 1)
Decision Thresholds
Set thresholds per metric category before the dark launch begins, not afterward. Post-hoc threshold selection is a form of p-hacking. A common threshold framework for GenAI systems:
Latency p99: shadow p99 must not exceed baseline p99 by more than 10% or a fixed ceiling (e.g., 500ms)
Error rate: shadow error rate must not exceed baseline by more than 0.5 percentage points
Quality score: shadow mean LLM-judge score must be within 0.1 points of baseline (on a 1–5 scale)
Cost: shadow cost per request must not exceed baseline by more than 20%
Minimum sample size: at least 1,000 paired observations at 80% statistical power before any gate is evaluated
Real-World Example

A team building a GenAI search system ran a dark launch to evaluate a new retrieval-augmented generation pipeline against the existing keyword-based baseline. The shadow system used a newer embedding model and an additional reranking step before LLM generation.

After 72 hours and 15,000 paired observations, the comparison report showed: quality score improved by 0.3 points (p < 0.001, Cohen's d = 0.42), p50 latency improved by 40ms, but p99 latency increased by 380ms due to the reranking step, and cost per request increased by 35% from the extra embedding call.

The team held the promotion. They optimized the reranking step with a cache layer and reduced cost by batching embedding calls, then re-ran the dark launch two weeks later. The second run passed all gates and was promoted to canary.
Common Mistakes
Comparing different time periods: production metrics from last week vs shadow metrics from this week conflates temporal drift with system differences
Ignoring variance in AI outputs: comparing means without testing distribution shape misses the non-determinism that makes LLM comparisons hard
Under-powered samples: declaring success after 100 observations when detecting small effect sizes requires thousands of samples for 80% power
Single-metric optimization: chasing quality score improvements while ignoring cost and latency degradations
Not validating the judge: using an LLM-as-judge without calibrating it against human ratings for the specific task domain
Missing tool call analysis: for agentic systems, failing to compare tool selection patterns misses efficiency regressions invisible in final output quality
Best Practices
Pre-register thresholds: document all promotion gates in a comparison spec before the dark launch begins
Sample stratified traffic: ensure shadow requests include both high-load and low-load periods, and diverse query types
Use a composite quality score: combine LLM-judge, embedding similarity, and task-specific metrics with documented weights
Track the judge itself: monitor judge model availability and score distribution over time; a drifting judge invalidates quality comparisons
Store raw comparison artifacts: save the full comparison report for post-launch audit and future calibration
Performance & Scalability

The comparison pipeline itself must process metrics in near-real-time if you want to detect regressions quickly during the dark launch. At 1,000 requests per minute, a nightly batch script will not catch a quality regression for many hours.
Stream processing: compute running statistics using Welford's online algorithm to avoid storing all historical latencies in memory
Sampling the judge: run LLM-as-judge on a random 5–10% sample of requests; full coverage is prohibitively expensive at scale
Columnar storage: store per-request metrics in Parquet or a time-series database for fast percentile queries
Statistical shortcuts: use the Mann-Whitney U test on reservoir samples instead of full-population paired tests at very high request volumes
Tooling & Ecosystem
Arize AI / Phoenix: purpose-built LLM observability with shadow run comparison views and built-in LLM-as-judge evaluation
LangSmith: LLM tracing and evaluation with experiment comparison across runs and datasets
Statsig / Optimizely: experimentation platforms with statistical significance calculators adapted for LLM metrics
RAGAS: open-source framework for RAG pipeline evaluation with faithfulness, answer relevance, and context precision scores
Prometheus + Grafana: infrastructure-layer metrics comparison with histogram and quantile aggregations for latency
Comparison with Alternatives
The table below contrasts dark launch metrics comparison against alternative evaluation approaches for AI systems.

Security & Reliability
Shadow traffic must not contain live credentials or PII when routed to a test stack — scrub or tokenize sensitive fields before mirroring
LLM-as-judge responses should not be stored in plaintext if they contain user query content; apply the same data retention policies as production responses
The comparison pipeline itself is a reliability risk — if it crashes, the dark launch produces no data; add health checks and alerting on the pipeline

Validate that the shadow system does not perform write operations (database mutations, email sends) triggered by shadow requests — shadow stacks must be read-only with respect to external side effects
Key Takeaways

Dark launch metrics comparison evaluates a candidate AI system against the production baseline across quality, latency, cost, and error dimensions simultaneously, using real traffic with zero user exposure

Paired statistical tests (paired t-test for latency, Wilcoxon for quality scores) are required because both systems process the same requests, enabling controlled comparisons that account for natural traffic variation
Cohen's d effect size must accompany every p-value — statistical significance without practical significance can lead to promoting changes that are technically measurable but operationally irrelevant
Quality comparison for generative AI requires LLM-as-judge, embedding similarity, and task-specific metrics in combination, because no single automated score captures all quality dimensions
Thresholds for all promotion gates must be pre-registered before the dark launch begins — post-hoc threshold selection is a form of p-hacking that invalidates the comparison
Shadow traffic must be temporally aligned with the production baseline window, and the shadow stack must be read-only to prevent duplicate side effects from mirrored requests
At high request volumes, stream statistical computation using Welford's algorithm and sample the LLM judge at 5–10% to keep the comparison pipeline efficient without sacrificing signal quality
